speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three of Fred Jackson on Monday July 25, 1924 in Beverly Hills, California. [SIDE CONVERSATION]

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very clear. Your stories are very clear. It's great. Anytime you want to start.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kochendorf, we arrived to Kochendorf. It was about a two-day train ride from Longwy to Kochendorf We already knew the Germans are in trouble because we could hear their cannons. We knew that the allies could not be maybe 10 or 20 kilometers away from us. You know, from the hearing because there were some experts that were in the military that were with us. I had a guy with the [NON-ENGLISH] was a captain. He says, I know. I was in the artillery. They are about 10 kilometers, maybe 15 away from us. They load us up one night, and they took us to Kochendorf. We arrived to Kochendorf. It was not Auschwitz because Auschwitz was, you know, we were not thirsty and everything. And we stayed there one day. Next day, it was work. And we did not know about this beating because in Longwy, basically there was no this type of beating. And I went marching, you know, on the first day, and, you know, there was an apple on the floor. I was hungry. I bend down. He said, this is the first time, I'm not going to kill you. He said this because the first day you did not know that anybody who bends down gets a shot in the head. And everybody spits out whatever you have and throw away everything. And one guy took an apple. He was in front of line and just put it under his shirt, and he seen it. He took him on the side, beat him to death. He just beat him to death with the butt of the rifle. He just beat him to death over that. We got the message.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from then on, there was no more-- worked and when we got down in the mine, they asked us, what kind of engineer are you? What kind of engineer are you? You know, chemical, this one? I told them general. What am I going to-- I wasn't an engineer. What was I? 20 years old? I wasn't an engineer, and my brother was 17 years old. General engineer. OK, general engineer, you're fine, stay here. So I became an electric welder. You know, laying on your back, no mask protection, and was welding the pipes. You know, some type of pipes. They showed you where. No, you didn't have to be a genius to do it. They have to show you once, you know what I mean? But my eye was killing me. As a matter of fact, to go back, when I came back to Prague, I was blind on one eye, left. And when I went to the doctor in Prague, he told me you're never going to see out of your left eye. He said never. That's gone, burned. The retina is burned, everything, and there's nothing I can do for you because glasses won't help either. And by miracle, a week later, I just opened my eye, and I seen. And I never had trouble since that. When I told the doctor here about it, I mean, here in the USA, he said well, this was a miracle because when the retina is burned you are blind. And he asked how did you burn your retina? So I told him. Oh, he says, that's different.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I worked three or four days, I worked in that kommando, the next day another commando, third day another, but very seldom with my brothers. I mean, very seldom. It happened maybe, through the months, maybe two, three times we were together. It was sheer luck what you-- you know what I mean, which group you got. You'd have better groups and the worse group. But like I could-- I worked in the mine digging for coal, digging for salt, carry heavy equipment, extremely heavy, physical work, which I was not equipped for. Now nobody was equipped. None of the guys who were there were equipped for it for the simple reason we didn't have the nourishment. We needed food. That was heavy work. So naturally, most of the people died. Most people died. And that went on, like I told you, four months, same routine, same routine. You hardly see anybody who you knew. New people were coming. There was constant replacement, constant replacement. It's like a roller coaster, you know, constant replacement. And the only advantage I had that if I had a little bread, which I want to save for later, I could give it to my brother and I knew he's not going to touch it, and he didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we trusted each other. Because most people, unfortunately, even the father couldn't trust the son or the son the father. They would eat them up. They would lie that they beat them and fell-- which, you know, exaggerate. So what can you do? They beat you, they beat you. You don't have no choice, you know what I mean? Because you couldn't have bread on you. Even if it was your own. They won't let you save. You eat what you get. And life was very heavy and many, many people, including me, I wouldn't mind to die at all, you know what I mean? What kept me alive, you know, I mean, that my brother was there and my younger brother was there, and I figured, you know what I mean, that somebody has to survive to tell the world what's going on because we didn't believe that the world knows it. Because we knew we lived in a civilized world that nobody go for it for no reason whatsoever. And that's what kept me alive. I'll be honest with you. The problems started after Kochendorf, all right? Same situation, the allies were advancing. They put us in a train towards Dachau. The train, after an hour stopped because the rails were bombed. We had to get out. And then there was about 3,000 of us, OK? And then the death march started, which I think we were near the city of Ulm, U-L-M. I think, because we seen some signs, Ulm. You know, as you walk under the freeway?

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was no more kapo. There was nothing, just SS, and their job was kill as much as they can, and they did. And they did. If you sat down, they shot you on the spot. If you fell down, tripped, let's say, shot you on the spot. Anything, any minor things they got rid of you. To remember, back Kochendorf, I just remembered one thing. You know, when you are in the barracks, and we slept four of us on one of those-- you know the one, two story. You know, one of those bunks, four of us. Now, after you went in, you could not go on the toilet. You cannot get out from the garage because if you stepped out of the garage, I mean from the barracks, they shoot you on premises.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my brother, he says I have to urinate. I have to urinate, but if you urinated inside, the kapo killed you because you may smell. You went outside, the SS kill you. You know what I mean? And the kapo just-- I remembered that very vividly-- he came in, and he said, and I don't want any urination here. You know what I mean? How could people hold it? My God, there were sick people already. And he went out. He slipped out, and one of the kapos caught him, brought him back and called up the SS. SS came. They put his head into the oven. This is Charlie, you know what I mean, who came with me to USA. Put it in the oven, and he got 10 whipping, 10, across, you know what I mean? We always laugh about it. Ironically, we always said it, you know what? It wasn't so bad. It could have been 15. You know what I mean? The good Jewish humor prevailed, and that was very important. You know what I mean? We could laugh at anything. You know what I mean? You could laugh at anything. When he got beat, he said, it could have been worse. When we got a little soup, it could have been worse. They could skip altogether. And you know, that's what kept alive. I remember laughing at things which were tragedy. Remember when there was the hanging in the compound, the SS told everybody everybody has to laugh loud. If you didn't laugh, you were a goner the next day, you know? They give to the commander and the one who took us to work to kill them. Now, can you imagine, they hang a person and you have to stand there like-- [CRYING] how, you know? Isn't it terrible? But I didn't feel that, which is unbelievable. Can you imagine feeling glad that a father who begs for his son's life, the son has to hang him? Just beyond any comprehension, beyond any human understanding.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry. I'm going back and forth, you know. I mean, but you understand how your mind works. And Longwy, they almost eliminated, I would say 80% of the so-called 500 engineers. Then as we went on to Longwy, I told you, so they stopped the train in Ulm, and there was the death march. I would say 150 kilometers. Took us three weeks, and we slept always on the side of the road in a forest. They give us a little hot water in the morning. That's all they had, and they give us a little bread and a little soup. That's all it was, very little. We were really starving. I mean, but I want to tell you something about starvation. The first day's bad, the second day is very bad, the third day is better, and after the fourth day, it's nothing. You don't feel any pain.

speaker: interviewer
text: But thirst, that's the pain. That's the killer. You know, we didn't have any-- the last five days, you know, we didn't have any water, any food, zero, zero, nothing and the thirst was a killer. But people eat, you know, worms, anything they find. I could not eat it. I remember one guy came to me, I found three, have one. There's no way in the world I could have eaten it. No way and they-- it's a luxury you know? He meant it well. You know what I mean? They took everything in their mouth. Greenery, I'm not even talking about. Anything but green from the [INAUDIBLE] they eat, you know? Like monkeys, they would eat. But thirst is terrible, terrible. It's much worse than hunger. Hunger goes away after three days, you know? It gets better. After the fourth day, I wasn't hungry anymore, but thirsty. And we finally arrived after so many was in Dachau. 180 people out of 3,000. On this march, were you all men? Were you men and women?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, all men, only men for the labor. But those were already from all over, from France, a lot of people. Belgium, you know, Italy, everywhere is was an international one. And there were all Jews, no exception. Because even before Dachau was liberated, two days before we got all the Jews together, only the Jews from Dachau, the non-Jews stayed there. They didn't take them away. I want you to understand. They didn't destroy them.

speaker: interviewer
text: All the Jews, they took us out under the pretenses that the International Red Cross came and we are going to Switzerland, OK? But between Dachau and Switzerland, which is about only 20, 30 kilometers, there was no regular train. You know, it was that, what do you call it? The cable cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cable cars, you know what I mean? And they couldn't put in more than 55, 75 people into it. And they took them, and when they came back, the SS came back, naturally and then they said, oh, that is Switzerland. We were very happy. We said, thank God, you know, and for what?

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said, we are taking all the Jews because they don't deserve to be here. The political figures we'll leave it. You know what I mean? Only the Jews. Anyway, they loaded the train, you know, for us, about 75 people, I think. And we're going about five-- They loaded the train or the cable car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cable car, cable car, there was no train, you know? Cable car, you know what I mean? And we were going about for ten minutes when the cable car stopped. They were bombing Munich, the allies, and obviously, the main station that gives the power wasn't. That's why I told you the [INAUDIBLE]. Anyway, they lowered us down, and the SS disappeared. They just disappeared. That was the last day of April. When we came down, you know, we crawled down, they left us down. There was no SS anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were on the field where the war was going on. We heard clearly machine guns shooting, you know? The US Army was coming in, we heard clearly, and we just stayed about one day on the field. And I was liberated with my brothers, two brothers, and we had a couple other friends. Five of us, I know, on the field.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now when we seen the first US soldiers, they behaved very nice. I want you to understand that. I don't care what they say. With us, they right away threw us all kind of food, cheeses, K rations, you know, oranges, bananas, you know, chocolate, a lot of chocolate, you know, threw out. Unfortunately, it didn't help most of the people because they died from it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from that place, I was there only one day on the field when the US Army gave us trucks. You know, they instructed give us trucks, and they took us to Garmisch-Partenkirchen. You know, that's a resort where the Olympics were. They took us there, and then they came there. Then they gave us, not only to us, the other groups that came over there the barracks, which used to be former SS. And that's what we occupied, OK? And there we were looked over by American doctors. There was a doctor over there. He was a major from Chicago, Dr. Meyer. And everybody has dysentery, terrible. And he told me, well, how is the food? I told him, it goes in, it comes out. I told him. That's it. It goes in, goes out. He said, well, that's no good. And he says to me, I told them that time, I think charcoal is very good. And it's charcoal. And there was charcoal over there, you know, because there was-- I mean, over there-- and we ate charcoal, and he gave me a morphine shot, too. Because he said morphine stops the dysentery, too. And you know what? It stopped it. That's why I'm here. That's why 90% who were liberated are not here, because of the food. Naturally, the Americans, it was good will. They didn't realize it, you know what I mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were liberated, like I told you, we went to Garmisch-Partenkirchen, and we had all the free access going in, going out. There was no more, you know-- I still had the striped suit on me because that's the only thing I had. And we went to a home. I figured, we go to a home, maybe I could find something to wear instead of what's there. And there was an old lady there, a German, actually a German old lady. And she said, don't kill me. I didn't know nothing. I didn't-- I didn't know, you know, my husband died on the Russian front. He was in the Wehrmacht, you know, and really honest, I don't know nothing. And you can have anything you want. I told her, we don't want anything. I'm looking for something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she stayed in the kitchen-- listen to this one. We went to the bedroom. I opened the drawer, SS uniform, Gestapo uniform. I mean, you know, like this, in order. Not just thrown in, you know what I mean? You could see it was just cleaned and ironed and everything over there. Can you imagine? It's an SS uniform. Then we went over there, and we told them, what is this? [SPEAKING GERMAN] I don't know. Nobody knew nothing. I want you to understand. No German knew there was a concentration camp, which is a lie. They all knew it. Some knew it more, some knew it less, but they all knew it because it was just in their background. This happened after the order, you know, after liberation. I stayed there about for six weeks in Garmisch-Partenkirchen. There's who speaks English, a volunteer and we were distributors of the Red Cross packages. You know what I mean? Cheese and every-- naturally I had all I wanted when I was there already but everybody had. There was no-- nobody died after liberation because of shortage, nobody. This is not true. Not at least who was liberated by the US Army, nobody. And stayed there and then they said, well, you want to go home? I go, sure, I want to go home, my mother is home. Even then you understand what a human being is? Even then, maybe somebody came home, maybe a cousin. Of course, I want to go home. Anyway, OK, they load us on a truck and they took us to [INAUDIBLE]. That was in Czechoslovakia, but it was one side American. The other side was the Russian army. We had to come down from the trucks. We were walking. We came on the side, and we had a little pack of chocolates or whatever they gave us. The Russian guard took it away. I told them, you know, we are Jews. We are just liberating. [SPEAKING RUSSIAN] you understand what I mean? I fought. Everything is mine. And I come back, you know, we need some food, something give us. They took us everything and I told him, how do we get to the station? Person said, walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, you know, that was no forced walk. We walked. The station was about two, three kilometers in, and we took the train. And we went-- from there you could not go to Prague because the roads were bombed. So we went through Hungary, Budapest, Debrecen, after this you probably don't know, Miskolc and then from then we went from train to Ungvar. That's how we got home. Naturally when we got home, we find out the [INAUDIBLE], and I stayed there. I knew my wife before the war. She used to go to school with my younger brother, Tibor, who died. And I knew when I stayed in Ungvar, I never dreamed that I'd not go away, but I've seen communism is just another form of fascism or Nazism. You can call it whatever you want to.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I left there everything, whatever I found from my parents. We came with a suitcase, and I went over to Czechoslovakia. I went to Prague. I went to the University of Prague. I had the credentials. I showed them. They said, well, what school do you want to go? Medicine is open. That was in July 1945, July, end of July. Medicine is open. Law school is open. No, law school is closed. It will not be open in what-- you know, the semester was filled. The first semester starts, the fall semester starts in September. He said, if you want to wait until February-- I told him, no, I don't want to wait. I don't have time. You had to pass a test. You took a written and oral test, you know, an entrance test. I passed it without any problem, and I didn't study. And I went to Prague University. I still have the [INAUDIBLE] with me, you know what I mean? With the picture and my identification card. And what happened, but how life, tragic life is. You know, we waited-- I applied right away for an American visa, 1945, and I waited end of '45, '46, '47. Two weeks before August 15, 1948, I received a letter from the American Consulate that, come in first of September. Your visa is ready to go, OK? Next day, I get from the army, a draft, you have to-- a draft, I got a draft that I have to report first of September in Prague this and this station. So a friend of ours, a doctor, much older than me, who came home in Prague, I told him. He said, look, Fred. I'll tell you want I'm going to do for you. [SOBBING] I'm risking my life. So are you. OK, you're going to them you have ulcers. When you have ulcers then they're going to give you six months later come back. Meanwhile, you have the visa, you don't come back, Anyway, I went and he told me how to act. Number one, he says, I was 100 pounds, you have to lose at least 10 to 15 pounds, That you should look really sick. Maybe I look really sick. I went into Prague, I go in front. Anybody-- you know, they asked you when you was there. Anybody who's sick stand up. So there were about four of us not Jews, you know what I mean? I stand up, and he says, oh, wait a minute, you are Fred Jakubovic? I told him, yes. He says, oh, no, you don't belong here. You're going to officer school. Officers, you know what I mean? You know, anybody went to university automatically officer school in Europe. I told them-- well, the difference was that if I would go here it's only two months. Officer school, you know, the basic training, two months and then you are out. You were in reserve. You know, you've been in it. Officer school is two years. How can I go in for two years? So I told them, I'm very sick. What's the matter? Ulcers, bleeding ulcers. How do you know it's bleeding? I told them I went to the doctor, this and this. He told me I have bleeding ulcers.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says to me, OK, we will take x-rays. Now, he told me if they take x-rays and you swallow that barium, we are cooked because there's no ulcer. So you have to do it so that you somehow manage not to taking the barium. So he gave me-- I had to come back in three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meanwhile, I went to US Consulate, and I got my visa. This all went on, remember, this was 1st of September, and 15th of September I was in the US. We did two weeks, everything. Can you imagine that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went there, and the doctor says, well, you have ulcers. Let me see. Take the barium, and then we send you to the hospital, you know, in the Prague hospital. And the doctor turned away. I stuck my finger in, I threw up everything before the barium had a chance to settle. There was no choice. Life and death.

speaker: interviewer
text: I threw it up and the doctor said, what's happening? I told him, I can't. He says uh-oh, your ulcer might be perforated. You're going to the hospital. I told him, do me a favor. Don't send me to the hospital. My wife is the only one who can cook for me. What do you eat? I told him farina. I love farina, you know what I mean, she's the one. Let her prepare it. I go to the hospital and I'll get sicker.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, OK, two weeks furlough After two weeks report back to us. We'll see if I can take it. And I went to US Consulate and another problem. I just looked at it last night, my passport was only ending September 30. The US Government will not give a visa unless the passport is six months valid.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so I went to the what's his name. Steinhardt was the American Ambassador in Prague. I went, I didn't talk to him. I talked to one of the deputies, and I told him, listen, this is the story. I'll tell you the truth. If I don't go now, I'm dead, I'm cooked. He said, stay here. He went in. And he obviously talked to Steinhardt or somebody, but I don't know whom. He came back. He said give me your passport. Put it in, he says, you know what I mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was the second, no, that must have been about the 5th or 6th of September 1948. And I landed in USA, 15th of September, a week later. Excitement, how many times can a person be killed. [LAUGHING] I felt like I'm a cat. How many times can I kill you, but this one was tremendous.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also when I was on the airport, I had a terrible experience. The immigration, the check, one guy came and said, this is your-- I had one suitcase. He said, what's in it? Open, clothes, junk. He said, oh no, this one's going to take me more than three hours, your plane leaves in three hours. To do it, you have to leave with the next plane. I knew there was no next plane. There was no next plane. I go, what shall I do? Well, at that minute, somebody comes to him and he tells him-- [CRYING] tells him, you know, you're wanted in the office. Why don't we change tapes, OK?
